name: Platform-Neutral Authentication Standards Adoption
consequences: Shifting focus to open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn reduces reliance on proprietary platform signals, but requires retraining public-sector developers and overcoming institutional inertia in legacy system integration. This creates a technical debt gap where existing MitID workflows remain incompatible with new standards without parallel modernization efforts.
options: Advocate for mandatory FIDO2 integration in all public-sector identity providers by 2028
Develop a cross-platform authentication SDK compatible with GrapheneOS and open-source Android forks
Lobby for eIDAS2 certification criteria that explicitly prioritize open-standard authentication mechanisms
review: Prioritizing open standards risks delaying immediate procurement influence as public-sector stakeholders resist rearchitecting existing systems, leaving a gap in transitional support for legacy infrastructure.

name: Hardware-Token Fallback Infrastructure Development
consequences: Creating a hardware-token-based fallback system enhances resilience against platform outages but introduces logistical challenges in distribution, user education, and secure key management. This approach may also face resistance from citizens accustomed to mobile-first authentication workflows.
options: Deploy a pilot program distributing open-hardware security keys to high-risk public-service users
Integrate hardware-token support into the AltID wallet architecture as a secondary authentication layer
Establish a national hardware-token certification body aligned with NSIS requirements
review: Hardware-token development diverts resources from software-based solutions, creating a gap in addressing immediate platform dependency while long-term hardware logistics are resolved.

name: Procurement Language Reform for Identity Systems
consequences: Amending procurement contracts to mandate platform-neutral authentication interfaces forces vendors to adapt, but requires sustained political capital to override existing supplier relationships. This creates a dependency on legislative timelines rather than technical innovation.
options: Insert mandatory interoperability clauses in all public-sector identity service tenders by 2027
Create a centralized procurement review board to audit platform dependency risks in identity contracts
Develop a standardized API framework for identity verification that bypasses platform-specific signals
review: Procurement reform depends on political will rather than technical progress, leaving a gap in enforcement mechanisms if institutional resistance persists.

name: EU-Level Standards Advocacy
consequences: Aligning Danish requirements with European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks amplifies influence but dilutes focus on national-specific needs. This risks creating a compliance-driven approach that prioritizes EU alignment over localized resilience strategies.
options: Propose platform-neutrality requirements in the European Digital Identity Wallet technical specifications
Form a Nordic working group to harmonize procurement standards across Baltic and Benelux states
Integrate fallback authentication criteria into the EU's upcoming eIDAS3 regulatory proposals
review: EU advocacy shifts control to supranational bodies, creating a gap in direct influence over Danish-specific implementation timelines and priorities.

name: Public-Private Coalition Mobilization
consequences: Building a coalition across civil society, academia, and industry strengthens advocacy power but introduces coordination challenges and conflicting priorities. This risks diluting the project's technical focus with broader political agendas.
options: Establish a multi-stakeholder advisory board with representatives from privacy groups, banks, and public-sector IT departments
Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting platform dependency risks through citizen case studies
Create a joint research initiative with Danish universities to publish independent audits of platform trust models
review: Coalition building consumes resources that could be directed toward technical development, leaving a gap in direct demonstration of fallback solutions without broader stakeholder buy-in.

name: Legacy System Interoperability Testing
consequences: Mapping MitID's dependency surface reveals critical integration points but exposes the complexity of retrofitting legacy systems. This creates a risk of overestimating the feasibility of incremental upgrades versus wholesale replacement.
options: Conduct a comprehensive audit of platform integrity signals in all public-sector identity workflows
Develop middleware solutions that translate platform-specific trust metrics into open-standard equivalents
Create a sandbox environment to test fallback authentication paths against legacy system architectures
review: Interoperability testing highlights technical complexity, creating a gap between theoretical feasibility and practical implementation timelines for public-sector systems.

name: Contingency Planning for Platform Policy Shifts
consequences: Preparing for potential changes in platform policies strengthens resilience but requires ongoing monitoring and resource allocation. This creates a dependency on continuous surveillance of external regulatory developments.
options: Establish a dedicated team to track and respond to changes in Apple/Google platform integrity policies
Develop a scenario-planning framework for worst-case platform withdrawal or restriction events
Create a reserve fund for rapid deployment of alternative authentication solutions during crises
review: Contingency planning diverts resources from proactive development, creating a gap in immediate progress toward platform-neutral solutions without urgent triggers.

name: Decentralized Identity Protocol Integration
consequences: Adopting decentralized identity protocols reduces reliance on platform-controlled trust models but introduces complexity in aligning with existing eIDAS and NSIS compliance frameworks. This approach risks fragmenting user experience if interoperability with legacy systems is not carefully managed.
options: Implement blockchain-based identity verification layers compatible with FIDO2 standards
Develop a national open-source identity federation protocol for cross-platform use
Integrate decentralized identifiers (DIDs) with existing MitID authentication flows
review: Prioritizing technical decentralization risks delayed adoption due to regulatory alignment challenges, leaving a gap in immediate operational continuity for critical services.

name: Local Tech Ecosystem Certification
consequences: Certifying domestic identity technology vendors strengthens supplier diversity but requires significant time and resources to establish credibility. This could delay immediate fallback solutions while building long-term resilience.
options: Create a national certification framework for local identity technology vendors
Establish a public-private partnership to audit and endorse domestic authentication solutions
Develop a tiered certification program for identity infrastructure components
review: Investing in local certification creates a dependency on slow regulatory processes, leaving a gap in urgent fallback capability during platform policy shifts.

name: Public-Private Identity Resilience Funds
consequences: Allocating dedicated funding for identity resilience initiatives fosters innovation but creates a dependency on sustained financial commitment. This could divert resources from other critical digital infrastructure priorities.
options: Launch a multi-year grant program for platform-neutral identity research and development
Create a public-private fund to subsidize alternative authentication infrastructure deployment
Establish a risk mitigation reserve for identity system contingency planning
review: Financial commitment requirements may limit scalability, leaving a gap in rapid response capabilities for unexpected platform disruptions.

name: Cross-Platform Authentication Standards
consequences: Developing universal authentication standards enhances interoperability but faces resistance from platform providers prioritizing proprietary ecosystems. This could slow adoption without strong regulatory backing.
options: Propose EU-wide authentication standardization requirements for public services
Collaborate with Nordic regulatory bodies to mandate platform-agnostic authentication interfaces
Pilot a multi-platform authentication framework for public-sector use cases
review: Standardization efforts risk stagnation without enforcement mechanisms, leaving a gap in practical implementation of platform-neutral solutions.

name: Citizen Awareness Campaigns
consequences: Educating citizens about identity resilience options builds long-term trust but requires sustained engagement. This may not directly address institutional procurement barriers or technical integration challenges.
options: Launch a national public education initiative on digital identity risks and alternatives
Partner with media outlets to create accessible identity security content
Develop community-based workshops for vulnerable user groups
review: Focus on awareness risks neglecting structural procurement reforms, leaving a gap in institutional adoption of alternative solutions.

name: Alternative Authentication Channel Development
consequences: Expanding authentication channels beyond mobile platforms increases accessibility but requires rethinking user onboarding and security protocols. This could strain existing identity verification processes.
options: Design hardware-token-based authentication workflows for non-smartphone users
Implement browser-based authentication for low-code public-service access
Develop SMS/voice-based fallback authentication for emergency scenarios
review: Diversifying channels creates integration complexity, leaving a gap in seamless user experience across different access methods.

name: National Identity Infrastructure Audits
consequences: Conducting comprehensive audits of identity systems identifies vulnerabilities but may reveal politically sensitive dependencies on foreign platforms. This could provoke resistance from incumbent stakeholders.
options: Perform third-party security audits of MitID's platform dependency surface
Map critical authentication pathways for public-sector services
Publish risk assessments of supplier concentration in identity infrastructure
review: Audit findings may face institutional pushback, leaving a gap in actionable mitigation strategies for identified risks.

name: Hardware-First Fallback Architecture Development
consequences: Developing hardware-based fallbacks reduces reliance on OS platforms but requires significant investment in secure, interoperable devices. This could strain budget allocations if not prioritized alongside software initiatives, potentially delaying broader adoption.
options: Deploy open-source hardware tokens integrated with existing Danish public services
Partner with local manufacturers to produce regionally certified authentication devices
Pilot hardware-based authentication in high-risk public sectors before general rollout
review: Prioritizing hardware infrastructure risks diverting resources from software resilience, leaving gaps in scenarios where physical devices are inaccessible or compromised.

name: Cross-Border Identity Resilience Agreements
consequences: Establishing cross-border agreements enhances interoperability with neighboring countries but introduces legal and regulatory complexity. This could slow down implementation if alignment with foreign standards proves contentious.
options: Negotiate bilateral identity verification accords with Nordic countries
Advocate for standardized fallback protocols within the EU Digital Identity Wallet framework
Create a regional consortium to harmonize authentication requirements
review: Cross-border agreements may dilute Denmark's ability to enforce strict sovereignty requirements, leaving gaps in unilateral control over critical authentication processes.

name: Open-Source Identity Protocol Adoption
consequences: Adopting open-source protocols increases transparency and reduces vendor lock-in but risks fragmentation if multiple incompatible systems emerge. This could complicate integration with existing infrastructure.
options: Develop a national open-source identity framework aligned with eIDAS standards
Integrate existing open-source solutions like FedCM or WebAuthn into public services
Fund community-driven development of decentralized authentication tools
review: Open-source adoption may lack the enterprise-grade support needed for critical services, creating vulnerabilities in maintenance and scalability.

name: Regional Authentication Infrastructure Partnerships
consequences: Forming regional partnerships diversifies authentication infrastructure but requires coordination across multiple stakeholders. This could lead to inconsistent implementation if not centrally managed.
options: Establish regional authentication hubs to share resources and expertise
Create joint procurement agreements for secure authentication hardware
Develop shared compliance frameworks for cross-regional identity verification
review: Regional partnerships might prioritize local interests over national sovereignty, creating disparities in authentication standards across Denmark.

name: Secure Research and Development Facilities Expansion
consequences: Expanding secure facilities enhances R&D capabilities but increases operational costs. This could limit the project's ability to scale other initiatives if budget constraints arise.
options: Lease additional secure workspaces in Copenhagen for collaborative development
Upgrade existing facilities with advanced cybersecurity measures
Partner with academic institutions to co-develop secure authentication technologies
review: Facility expansion may divert attention from external advocacy efforts, leaving the project vulnerable to political and regulatory challenges.

name: Legislative Framework for Digital Sovereignty
consequences: Drafting new legislation strengthens digital sovereignty but faces political resistance from stakeholders reliant on existing platforms. This could delay implementation and reduce effectiveness.
options: Propose amendments to existing digital governance laws to mandate platform neutrality
Create a parliamentary committee to oversee digital identity policy reforms
Introduce incentives for public-sector agencies to adopt non-platform-dependent solutions
review: Legislative efforts may struggle to pass without broad political support, leaving gaps in enforceable standards for authentication practices.

name: Community-Driven Identity Solutions
consequences: Encouraging community involvement fosters innovation but may lack the scale needed for national implementation. This could result in fragmented solutions that don't address systemic dependencies.
options: Launch a public challenge to develop decentralized identity tools
Support grassroots initiatives for local authentication networks
Create a platform for citizen feedback on identity infrastructure improvements
review: Community-driven approaches may not align with institutional requirements, leading to solutions that are innovative but impractical for large-scale deployment.
